abstract: GROMACS has established itself as a premier engine for biomolecular simulation, evolving over two decades from a tool centered on single-core efficiency to a highly sophisticated framework capable of utilizing the world’s largest supercomputers. This paper explores the architectural and algorithmic transformations required to navigate the challenges of the exascale era. We detail the implementation of multi-level parallelism, which integrates wide SIMD acceleration, high-performance GPU offloading, and a hybrid MPI/OpenMP execution model. These advancements necessitated a fundamental redesign of molecular dynamics kernels—transitioning to a cluster-based neighbor-searching algorithm that maximizes throughput on both modern CPUs and accelerators. Beyond intra-node performance, we address strong-scaling bottlenecks through task decomposition and the management of long-range interactions, while highlighting the role of ensemble simulation frameworks in saturating massive computational resources. Finally, we discuss the evolution of the GROMACS development process, including the transition to C++, the integration of rigorous peer review, and continuous testing protocols essential for maintaining a robust, performance-oriented open-source codebase for the scientific community.
